name: Uraeus
shortDescription: The Uraeus is an upright cobra, often depicted on the brow of Egyptian rulers, symbolizing sovereignty, royalty, deity, and divine authority.
description: The Uraeus (plural: Uraei) is a stylized, upright form of an Egyptian cobra (likely the Egyptian cobra, *Naja haje*). It was used as a symbol of sovereignty, royalty, deity, and divine authority in ancient Egypt. Most prominently, the Uraeus adorned the brow of pharaohs, representing their legitimacy and protection. The cobra was associated with the goddess Wadjet, the patron deity of Lower Egypt, and its presence on the royal headdress signified the pharaoh's control over both Upper and Lower Egypt. The Uraeus wasn't merely ornamental; it was believed to possess potent protective powers, spitting fire at the enemies of the pharaoh and the sun god Ra. In this capacity, it acted as a guardian, warding off evil and maintaining cosmic order (Ma'at). The Uraeus could also represent other powerful goddesses, such as Bastet or Sekhmet, emphasizing its connection to divine power and protection. Its presence was not limited to the pharaoh; Uraei could also adorn the crowns of gods and goddesses, signifying their own sovereignty and power. Its association with the sun god Ra further cemented its importance in the Egyptian pantheon, linking earthly power with divine authority and the eternal cycle of life and death. The image of the Uraeus served as a constant reminder of the pharaoh's divine right to rule and their responsibility to uphold Ma'at.

sources.title: The Gods of the Egyptians, Vol 1
sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
